terms.definition: The capacity of a social system to keep its constituent parts functionally aligned; operationalised here as the ratio of inward to outward attention vectors.
Information-theoretic entropy: the expected value of surprise in a message stream, used here as a proxy for social disorder.
A semi-permeable boundary that selectively filters flows (energy, matter, information) to maintain local order.
A directional allocation of cognitive, emotional, and logistical resources toward either local (inward) or external (outward) referents.
terms.first_use_section: The Coherence Metric: Inward vs. Outward Vectors

terms.definition: The study of how human attention is treated as a scarce commodity in digital markets.
terms.first_use_section: The Coherence Metric: Inward vs. Outward Vectors

terms.definition: An estimated cognitive limit (~150) on the number of stable social relationships an individual can maintain.
An embodied system of dispositions shaped by one’s social environment and practices.
A period of adolescent social testing in which Amish youth may explore life outside community norms before choosing adult baptism.
A foundational proposition accepted within a theoretical framework from which further structural claims are derived.
A system’s capacity to maintain control over the pathways by which external forces can exert influence.
